questions.question: A horizontal force of $8\text{ N}$ pushes a trolley $5\text{ m}$ across a floor. Calculate the work done on the trolley.
questions.explanation: $W = F \times s = 8\text{ N} \times 5\text{ m} = 40\text{ J}$. **Common mistake:** Dividing force by distance ($1.6$) instead of multiplying.

questions.question: A student lifts a $4\text{ kg}$ box vertically through a height of $1.5\text{ m}$. Calculate the work done against gravity. (Take $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
questions.explanation: Work done against gravity $= mgh = 4\text{ kg} \times 10\text{ N/kg} \times 1.5\text{ m} = 60\text{ J}$. **Common mistake:** Using the mass directly without multiplying by $g$ to find the weight ($4 \times 1.5 = 6$).

questions.question: A force of $40\text{ N}$ is used to push a trolley a distance of $10\text{ m}$ up an incline. What is the mechanical work done by this force?
questions.explanation: Work is force \times the distance moved along the line of action of the force. $40\text{ N} \times 10\text{ m} = 400\text{ J}$. **Common mistake:** Trying to use the vertical height (which is not provided here) instead of the distance moved along the incline.

questions.question: To convert a work value from kilojoules ($\text{kJ}$) to the SI unit joules ($J$), you should:
questions.explanation: The prefix 'kilo' means 1000, so $1\text{ kJ} = 1000\text{ J}$. **Common mistake:** Dividing by 1000, which is the conversion from $J$ to $kJ$.

questions.question: A box is pulled $3.0\text{ m}$ across a smooth floor by a horizontal force, doing $24\text{ J}$ of work. What is the magnitude of the force?
questions.explanation: Rearranging $W = Fs  gives F = W/s = 24\text{ J} / 3.0\text{ m} = 8.0\text{ N}$. **Common mistake:** Multiplying work and distance to get^72.
